transcript.text: If a book ever makes you feel stupid,
that might mean that you have chosen the
right book.
Most people stop reading the moment they
become confused. They feel like this is
too hard for me. But confusion might
just be the most valuable part of
reading. I wrote a post on X recently
and it was quite wellreceived which
surprised me because the idea is very
simple. I'm no genius. I'm 42 years old.
I have no higher education. And I'm not
a fast learner at all. But after reading
hundreds of books, uh, something
happened to me. Books that once
intimidated me don't anymore. There is a
quote from Naval Ravikant that explains
this perfectly. He says, quote, when
you're reading a book and you're
confused, that confusion is similar to
the pain that you get in the gym when
you're working out, but you're building
mental muscles instead of physical
muscles. Most people treat confusion as
a stop sign, but it is actually the
workout. When I first started to read,
like seriously, 11 or so years ago, I
was mostly reading quite accessible
books about habits, productivity,
popular psychology, and there's
absolutely nothing wrong with reading
those types of books. But after a while,
I felt curious about trying something
more challenging. So that's what I did.
And the first time I started to read
books like these, I felt uh completely
lost. There were pages uh where I
thought I have absolutely no idea what
this this means. Uh I know behave was a
behemoth of a book that
I went through. Games People Play. It
was a long time since I read it, but I
know it was probably 50% of the examples
in the books that I didn't fully get.
David Deutsches, the beginning of
infinity, quantum mechanics, advanced
physics. This was way out of my comfort
zone. Uh William James book, The Variety
of Religious Experience. Just the topic
and subject matter was outside of my
comfort zone. Old books like Plato's The
Republic felt very intimidating. How
would I understand what an old and very
wise old Greek man and master
philosopher was trying to say? Sigman
Freud just the name is intimidating
right Aristotle thus spoke of
Saratustra. This was a really
challenging book for me. I had to have
video companions. I was watching a video
series along with each chapter that
explained what he was actually talking
about.
Foust. Uh yeah, there's tons of books
like this. When these books got me
confused, I assumed the problem was with
me that I just wasn't smart enough. But
something interesting happened when I
kept reading away. The books, they
didn't change, but my brain started to
adapt to these new ideas. Think about
how the gym works. You don't want to
lift weights that feel easy. You want to
lift weights that feel slightly too
heavy. That's how you get the gains. And
it works the same way with reading.
Difficult books forces you to slow down,
to reread sections, think hard, connect
ideas. That mental effort is what
actually builds understanding. And here
is the fascinating part. It compounds
over time. You keep reading challenging
books. Your brain gets used to
complexity. Ideas that once felt
impossible start to feel manageable. Not
because the books got easier, but
because you got mentally stronger. After
years of reading like this, I noticed
something interesting. Almost no book
intimidates me anymore. Not because I
understand everything, but because I'm
comfortable with being confused. I trust
the process. And here is a quick test.
Think about the last five books you
read.
Pause the video, take a paper and pen
and write them down or write it in the
comment sections. Good. Are you done?
Now ask yourself, was any of these books
difficult? Did they force you to reread
certain pages? Did they introduce
concepts that you didn't fully grasp? If
the answer is no, then maybe you're
staying too much inside of your reading
comfort zone. Here's a rule that helped
me a lot. Every year, pick a few books,
maybe three of them that are slightly
outside of your depth. Not books that
are impossible, but books that forces
you to stop and think. Books that you
can maybe expect to only understand 60
70% the first time. Accept that. That's
normal. Understanding compounds over
time. I do this little simple trick
myself every year. And if you've seen my
2026 reading list, uh you can judge me
on my picks for this year and see if I
live up to the teachings that I teach
here on the channel. But this is a
concept that really have helped me a lot
to develop as a reader, as a person, as
a parent, as a leader. The goal is not
to feel smart. The goal is to become
smarter over time. And that, my friends,
often starts with a book that makes you
feel confused. Don't let the confusion
have you shy away from these types of
books. Books that will actually give you
the most growth possible. Uh that's what
was my little talk for today. I hope you
feel like you're ready to pick up that
one book that you have been wanting to
read but uh shied away because you were
intimidated. Thank you for tuning in.
I'm back next week with more reading
tips, book reviews, all of that. Until
then, you're out.
